coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots modified polyvinylidenefluride (PVDF) nanofibrous membranes with enhanced performance for air Gap membrane distillation
coredata.prism:publicationName: Chemical Engineering and Processing - Process Intensification
coredata.dc:creator: Jafari, Arman
Kebria, Mohammad Reza Shirzad
Rahimpour, Ahmad
Bakeri, Gholamreza
coredata.dc:description: This study investigates the potential of novel graphene quantum dots (GQDs)/polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) nanofibrous membranes for water desalination via air gap membrane distillation (AGMD) process. Different contents of GQDs were loaded in/on nanofibrous membrane for obtaining the desired structure for AGMD process. The resultant GQDs/PVDF nanofibrous presented a slight decrease in water contact angle and considerable increase in liquid entry pressure of water (LEPw). The sample containing 0.25 wt.% GQDs exhibited porosity of >90%, LEPw of >135 kPa, mean average surface roughness of 189.24 nm and water contact angle of >125°. Furthermore, the BET analysis exhibited that the mean pore diameter and effective surface area of nanofibrous membrane were modified at presence of GQD. AGMD experiments indicated that the higher water flux of 17.6 kg/m2 h and salt rejection of about 99.7% were achieved for GQD3P membrane containing 0.25 wt% GQDs compared to neat PVDF nanofibrous membrane with water flux of about 14.7 kg/m2 h and salt rejection of about 94.5% (partial wetting occurred after 4 h) during filtration of 3.5 wt.% NaCl solution for 8 h under feed and permeate temperatures of 60 °C and 20 °C, respectively. Eventually, GQD3P exhibited the water flux of 10.8 kg/m2h and reliable salt rejection of >99.5% over 60 h experiment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Nanofibrous membrane
Desalination
Air gap membrane distillation

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Chemical Engineering and Processing - Process Intensification

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Interaction between PVDF chains and GQDs
BET and surface properties of the membranes
Morphological and topological properties of the membranes
Surface chemical properties of the nanofibrous membranes
Performance evaluation of PVDF and GQDs modified PVDF nanofibrous membranes in AGMD process

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Liquid Entry Pressure of water (LEPw) set-up.
TEM image of the synthesized GQDs (a), possible chemical structure of GQDs (b), and XRD measurements of GQDs nanoparticles (c).
Nitrogen adsorption/desorption diagram (a), and BJH pore size distribution (b) of neat PVDF and GQD3P nanofibrous membrane.
Cross sectional SEM images of the neat PVDF, GQD2P and GQD3P (a–c) and surface SEM images of the neat PVDF, GQD2P and GQD3P (d–f).
Two dimensional AFM images of neat PVDF (a) and GQD3P nanofibrous membrane (b).
FTIR spectra (a), and XRD (b) of neat PVDF and GQD3P nanofibrous membranes.
The stress-strain diagrams for neat PVDF and GQD3P nanofibrous membranes.
AGMD experiments results; short-term salt rejection (a), short-term water vapor flux (b), and long-term salt rejection (c), and long-term water vapor flux (d).
The calculated energies of the components (kcal/mol).
The characteristics of neat PVDF and GQDs/PVDF nanofibrous membranes.
Comparison of AGMD performance of different PVDF membranes.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots modified polyvinylidenefluride (PVDF) nanofibrous membranes with enhanced performance for air Gap membrane distillation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, Babol Noushirvani University of Technology, Shariati Ave., Babol, Iran

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study investigates the potential of novel graphene quantum dots (GQDs)/polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) nanofibrous membranes for water desalination via air gap membrane distillation (AGMD) process. Different contents of GQDs were loaded in/on nanofibrous membrane for obtaining the desired structure for AGMD process. The resultant GQDs/PVDF nanofibrous presented a slight decrease in water contact angle and considerable increase in liquid entry pressure of water (LEPw). The sample containing 0.25 wt.% GQDs exhibited porosity of >90%, LEPw of >135 kPa, mean average surface roughness of 189.24 nm and water contact angle of >125°. Furthermore, the BET analysis exhibited that the mean pore diameter and effective surface area of nanofibrous membrane were modified at presence of GQD. AGMD experiments indicated that the higher water flux of 17.6 kg/m2 h and salt rejection of about 99.7% were achieved for GQD3P membrane containing 0.25 wt% GQDs compared to neat PVDF nanofibrous membrane with water flux of about 14.7 kg/m2 h and salt rejection of about 94.5% (partial wetting occurred after 4 h) during filtration of 3.5 wt.% NaCl solution for 8 h under feed and permeate temperatures of 60 °C and 20 °C, respectively. Eventually, GQD3P exhibited the water flux of 10.8 kg/m2h and reliable salt rejection of >99.5% over 60 h experiment.

ce:section.ce:para: Water scarcity is becoming a major concern in the world. Seawater as huge resource of water needs to be desalinated to provide fresh water. Although, thermal desalination and reverse osmosis (RO) are the most common technologies to desalinate seawater, alternative techniques such as membrane distillation (MD) have presented superior potential for desalination industries [1]. MD is a thermal membrane process which is more energy efficient and simpler than conventional desalination methods. The main advantages of MD are lower operating pressure compared to RO and lower operating temperature compared to multi-stage flash (MSF) which allows to use low-grade or solar thermal energies and medium mechanical requirements [2–5]. There are four MD configurations to collect permeated vapor based on recovering procedure: 1) direct contact membrane distillation (DCMD), 2) air gap membrane distillation (AGMD), 3) sweeping gas membrane distillation (SGMD), and 4) vacuum membrane distillation (VMD) [6–13]. DCMD is the simplest kind of MD categories where the hot feed and permeate fluid are in contact with membrane surfaces [14,15]. Because of direct contact between hot and cold streams, heat loss in DCMD is much more than the other configurations. On the other hand, SGMD and VMD need condenser to convert the permeated vapor to liquid which makes these configurations complicated. Among these MD systems, AGMD has more potential for commercialization due to the stagnant air layer which lessen heat loss because of the less thermal conductivity of air compared to the other used materials [16–18]. In addition, the effect of temperature polarization phenomenon on AGMD performance is lower than DCMD due to the lower heat transfer coefficient of air. Despite of these superior characteristics, flux in AGMD is lower than DCMD or VMD due to the higher mass transfer resistance which is an important challenge that must be considered.

However, MD technology suffers from three main issues: 1) optimized module design, 2) energy requirement and 3) special membrane necessity [16–19]. Some studies have been investigated to develop proper module through utilizing spacers or obtaining more membrane area with less occupied space [18]. To overcome energy consumption problem, using waste-heat or solar power and the heat recovery devices could be helpful. MD needs a special membrane with surface hydrophobicity, high porosity, acceptable liquid entry pressure (LEP) and good thermal resistance. A hydrophobic porous membrane as a separator plays a key role in MD process where the hot brine passes on the feed side and only water vapor is allowed to permeate. So far, most of MD experiments have been done by commercially microfiltration and ultrafiltration hydrophobic membranes produced by phase inversion method [1,20–23]. However, there is still need to fabricate an appropriate membrane which possess high hydrophobicity and high LEP as well as high porosity.

In recent years, electrospinning has been employed to fabricate novel MD membranes. Using high voltage source to provide electric field leads to formation of fiber from polymer solution. Membranes fabricated by electrospinning method possess high surface area and hydrophobicity as well as high surface porosity. Essalhi et al. [24] studied the influence of electrospinning operating conditions on the properties of self-standing polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) nanofiber membranes. Lalia et al. [25] investigated the effects of heat-press post-treatment on the performance of nanofibrous membrane. Zhu et al. [26] fabricated a nanofibrous membrane with lotus-leaf like structure by polyaniline/polystyrene which presented appropriate hydrophobicity and conductivity. Furthermore, Hammami et al. fabricated ethylene-pentafluorophenylene modified poly(ether imide) nanofiber membranes with flux of about 140% higher than commercial membranes [27]. In order to enhance the membrane performance, nowadays, various groups of materials like zeolites and carbon-based nano-structured materials are being used [28–31]. Graphene based materials such as graphene oxide (GO) have attracted more attention due to their natural structure which can help water to permeate [32–36]. Graphene oxide has also the ability to make the composites stable with polymers [37]. Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) are nano-scaled pieces of graphene which exhibit exclusive properties, which let them to be an exciting candidate for a whole range of novel applications. Even though, GQDs are the graphene sheets smaller than 100 nm, but generally, they are smaller in size than GO [38–40]. As one of the carbon-based materials, GQDs are abundantly available, chemical inert, non-toxic, well dispersed material in polar solvents with the ability of intra-molecular bonding formation via their edges with various functional groups. These features of GQDs suggest its great potential to be used as the modification agent in various applications [39,41,42]. Zhang et al. fabricated nanofibrous membrane using GQDs into the polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) solution to investigate the fluorescence properties in determination of hydrogen peroxide and glucose [40]. Small size of GQDs can cause to better dispersion of its particles in polymer solution and therefore to form a membrane with more uniform structure. As an objective of this study, different types of nanofibrous membranes containing various contents of GQDs were fabricated and their desalination potential to produce fresh water from saline water was investigated by AGMD. The mechanical, structural and morphological properties of the electrospun membranes were also analyzed.

PVDF powder (Kynar® 761) as dope polymer and N, N-Dimethyl formamide (DMF) as solvent were supplied by Arkema Inc., France and Scharlau, Spain respectively. Also, sodium chloride (NaCl) was purchased from Merck. To synthesize GQDs, citric acid (CA) was provided by Merck.

In this study, GQDs were synthesized by direct pyrolysis of CA. Initially, 2 g CA was heated to 200 °C using mantle. After 5 min heating, the solid CA changed to a colorless liquid. Then, the colorless liquid turned to a yellow liquid by further heating and it became orange after 30 min which was a evidence for the formation of GQDs. After that, the liquid was cooled down to room temperature and filtered by a porous membrane (molecular weight cutoff 1 kDa) to dispel residual CA and then dried to achieve solid GQDs.

GQDs/PVDF solution was prepared by dissolving certain amounts of the materials in DMF. GQDs with different contents (0.05, 0.1, 0.25 and 0.5 wt.%, named as GQD1P, GQD2P, GQD3P and GQD4P, respectively) was dispersed in DMF through stirring for 1 h at stirring speed of 300 rpm and further 10 min sonication for better dispersion. PVDF (16 wt.%) was added to the mixture and then stirred at 50 °C overnight to obtain a homogeneous solution. A lab scale electrospinning unit (Fan Avaran Nano-Meghyas, Electroris, Iran) was used to produce electrospun membrane. To prepare electrospun nanofiber membranes, the polymeric solution was poured into a syringe and put in its certain location on syringe pump. Then, the polymeric nanofibers were spun directly onto a rotating drum covered by aluminum foil. 6 mL of polymeric solution was spun at 24 °C, relative humidity of 60% and mass flow rate of 0.5 ml/h using a 10 ml syringe with a “18 gauge” needle. To establish an electric field, a high voltage (24 kV) was used between the needle and the rotating drum (550 rpm) and an automatic system was applied to move the syringe pump in axial direction to fabricate a membrane with uniform structure. Finally, the produced membranes were put in an oven at 80 °C for 30 min to remove residual solvents [43]. No further treatment was done before experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interaction between PVDF chains and GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: A molecular dynamic simulation was done to study the possible interaction energy between PVDF nanofibrous membrane and GQDs. So, an amorphous cell model including PVDF chains and GQDs nanoparticles possessing COOH, CH2COOH and OH functional groups, was simulated to obtain the total energy of the mixture and each parts of the mixture, separately. All computations were performed by Materials Studio software and COMPASS force field using 6–9 Lennard-Jones potential equation to calculate the van der Waals interactions. Charge Qeq was applied to adjust the electrostatic charges of the atoms. Smart minimizer method, Ewald summation method with an accuracy of 10−5 kcal/mol, and atom based method with cutoff distance of 13 Å were chosen for energy minimization, electrostatic interaction calculation and van der Waals interaction computation, respectively. Furthermore, simulation at NPT (number of particles, pressure and temperature are constant) ensemble at 298.15 K under pressure of 1 bar for 2 ns was carried out by using Nose and Berendsen algorithms to control the temperature and pressure, respectively.

The morphologies of GQDs/PVDF nanofibrous membranes were analyzed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, VEGA\TESCAN-XMU). The surface and cross section of the samples were coated by gold and an accelerating voltage of 15 KV was applied to obtain the images. Membrane surface topology and roughness were investigated by atomic force microscopy (AFM, NANOSURF AG, Switzerland). The mean roughness of the surface (Ra) was also obtained. To assess the surface hydrophobicity of GQDs/PVDF electrospun membranes, equal sizes of (4 cm × 2 cm) samples were analyzed by a water contact angle measuring device (Sharif solar, CA-500M, Iran) using sessile drop method and then the data was obtained by measuring software. To exhibit the presence of GQDs into the matrix of electrospun membranes, Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy analysis (FTIR, BFRL WQF-510A, China) was carried out in the range of 460 to 4200 cm−1. X-Ray Diffraction (XRD, Panalytical, X’Pert Pro) analysis was carried out over angles varying from 5° to 80° (Cu Kα, λ = 1.54059 Å). The mechanical properties of the nanofibrous membranes were investigated by tensile tester device (HOUNSFIELD, H10KS, UK). The analysis was carried out under ambient conditions using constant elongation velocity of 5 mm/min.

Also, total pore volume, specific surface area and mean pore radius were measured using the Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) and the Barrett–Joyner–Ha-lenda (BJH) methods. Liquid Entry pressure of water (LEPw), as a parameter representing ability of hydrophobic membrane to resist against pore wetting, was measured using a dead-end LEP cell (Fig. 1 ). A membrane sample with surface areas of 29 cm2 was fixed in the membrane cell and the reservoir was filled with distilled water. Then, the hydraulic pressure was applied and raised stepwise with a stabilization period of 5 min for each 0.2 bar increase in pressure. As the first water drop was observed on the other side of the membrane, the pressure value was recorded as the LEPw. All the measurements were performed two times to report the errors.

Membrane porosity defined as the void volume of the membrane to the total volume, was measured using gravimetric procedure [44]. Membrane samples with scale of 3cm × 3 cm were prepared and dipped into the ethanol for 30 min. The membrane porosity (ε) is calculated through Eq. (1), using the weight of dry and wet membranes.
(1)
ε =
( W
w
-
W d
) /
D
E t
[
( W
w
-
W d
) /
D
E t
] +
W d
/
D p
Where Ww
W w
and Wd are the weights of the wet and dry membranes, DEt is the density of the ethanol and Dp is the density of the polymer (PVDF). Triplicate measurements were done to decrease the errors.

The neat PVDF and GQDs/PVDF electrospun membranes were placed into the home-made AGMD cell with the effective area of 32 cm2 and 2 mm air gap width. Fig. 2 shows the schematic of the AGMD set-up. NaCl solution (3.5 wt.%, conductivity = 60 mS/cm) as feed solution was heated to 60 °C and passed over the membrane surface and tap water with constant temperature of 20 °C was used as coolant on the permeate side. The feed flow rate was maintained at 30 L/h and the conductivity of permeate and feed was recorded invariably using conductivity meter (AZ, 86503).

Functional groups such as hydroxyl groups in GQDs structure and fluoride atoms in polymer chains can cause reliable interaction. The interaction was computed by molecular dynamic simulation and the total energy is calculated according to Eq. (2) [45]:
(2)
E
i n t
=
E
M i x
- (
E
P o l
+
E
G Q D s
)
Where Eint , EMix , EPol and EGQDs are interaction energy, total energy of the mixture, total energy of the polymer after elimination of GQDs and total energy of GQDs after elimination of the polymer, respectively. The amounts of different energies which have been calculated by molecular dynamic simulation are listed Table 1 . The negative sign of Eint is related to the reduction of mixture energy that indicates strong interaction between GQDs and the polymer chains.

Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM, Philips, CM120) image of the synthesized GQDs is displayed in Fig. 3 a. The TEM image of the synthesized GQDs confirmed uniform particle size distribution and approximately spherical morphology. Fig. 3b shows the possible chemical structure of GQDs. XRD measurement was performed to confirm complete formation of GQDs (Fig. 3c). XRD analysis for GQDs nanoparticles showed a strong broad pick at 26.2° indicating the amorphous stacking structure of graphene [46].

ce:section.ce:section-title: BET and surface properties of the membranes
ce:section.ce:para: To calculate the surface areas of GQD3P and the neat PVDF nanofibrous membrane, nitrogen adsorption/desorption method and BET analysis were used (Fig. 4 ). As can be seen in Fig. 4a, the adsorption and desorption diagrams for GQD3P membrane were over lapped and no hysteresis was observed whereas the adsorption/desorption curve for the neat PVDF membrane showed hysteresis at lower relative pressures indicating a large number of pores in the mesoporous region [47,48]. It is noted that the nitrogen adsorption/desorption curves represented a typical type II isotherm. Based on the analysis, the BET surface area and the mean pore size were 9.331 m2/gr and 4.9 nm for GQD3P and 5.701 m2/gr and 5.96 nm for the neat PVDF membrane. Fig. 4b shows the pore size distribution of the membranes based on the BJH analyzing method where in case of neat PVDF membrane, the pores with the radius of 1.65 nm have the maximum frequency; the higher the pore size, the lower the number of pores. By this behavior, it could be inspired that a large number of membrane pores are in mesopore region. On the other hand, for GQD3P membrane, pore size distribution had a growing trend all over the radius domain which shows smaller pore size, confirming nitrogen adsorption/desorption results. Also, the GQD3P had higher total pore volume than the neat PVDF membrane. This result in conjunction with the MD test results indicates that the membrane with smaller pore size would have better performance in MD process.

The membrane surface characteristics such as LEPw, porosity and water contact angle are presented in Table 2 . The analysis revealed that the fabricated nanofibrous membranes possessed highly porous structure while having acceptable water contact angle and LEPw. Furthermore, all of the membranes had a thickness around 200 μm. Nevertheless, a slight decrease could be observed in GQDs/PVDF membranes attributing to the increasing of GQDs/PVDF solution conductivity [46].

The water contact angle analysis showed that the tendency of the membrane surface to repel the water droplet did not change much even with the presence of low contents of GQDs in the PVDF matrix. As shown in Table 2, the water contact angle decreased sllightly by adding hydrophilic GQDs nanoparticles into membranes that can be related to the presence of hydrophilic functional group.

The decrease in the porosity of electrospun membranes from 94.11 for neat PVDF to 90.66 for GQD4P could be ascribed to pore blockage caused by the presence of GQDs nanoparticles and formation of bead in the fiber structure which might reduce the membrane performance in terms of water flux. High porosity is an effective factor in MD process due to its role in the vapor permeation through the membrane walls [49]. Considering the mean pore size of the fabricated membranes obtained from BET analysis and the water contact angle, GQDs/PVDF nanofibrous membranes showed an increasing trend in terms of LEPw from 0.05 wt.% to 0.25 wt.%. This trend could be attributed to the lower mean pore size at higher GQDs loading. However, a decline in LEPw was observed for GQD4P membrane which is mainly related to its lower hydrophobicity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological and topological properties of the membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The surface and cross sectional images of the neat PVDF and GQDs/PVDF membranes are shown in Fig. 5 . Based on the cross sectional images, the distance between the fibers decreased by increasing the content of GQDs that can lead to the improvement of mechanical properties within the experiments and lessen the membrane thickness (Fig. 5a–c). The surface SEM images of the nanofibrous membranes showed cylindrical and smooth fibers. Average diameter of the fibers are provided in Table 2. The diameter of nanofibers was decreased at presence of GQDs, which could be due to the increased conductivity with increasing GQDs content. This led to more charges for the solution and consequently introduce thinner fibers with more elongation. In addition, the images depicted uniform surface structure and the least beads-on-string which could be attributed to accurate electrospinning process and well dispersion of the GQDs particles in the polymeric solution. There were no obvious changes on the surface structure of PVDF and GQDs/PVDF electrospun membranes, indicating the existence of GQDs only influences on the chemical properties of fiber surface.

The topography of the electrospun membrane surface was investigated by AFM. Generally, based on the nature of nanofibrous membrane, these types of membranes have rougher surface than the membranes made by phase inversion method due to overlapping of fibers [50]. Fig. 6 shows the two dimensional AFM images of the neat PVDF and GQD3P nanofibrous membranes. The images depicted that the overlapping of fibers made ridges and valleys on the membrane surface. The GQD3P membrane showed higher mean surface roughness (303.25 nm) than neat PVDF membrane (100.28 nm). In addition to the overlapping structure, further roughness could be attributed to the presence of GQDs in the nanofibrous membrane matrix. Some beads were formed on the membrane by incorporating GQDs into the polymeric matrix. The presence of these beads will increase the surface roughness of formed GQDs/PVDF nanofibrous [49,50].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface chemical properties of the nanofibrous membranes
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 7 shows the FTIR and XRD analysis of the neat PVDF and GQD3P nanofibrous membranes. As can be seen in Fig. 7a, the peak at 1400 cm−1 corresponds to CH2 wagging or CF stretching vibrations for the neat PVDF [51]. Considering the existence of OH and COOH functional groups, the peaks at 1425 and 1379 cm−1 attributed to asymmetric and symmetric stretching of carboxylate and a weak broadened peak at 3304 cm−1 can be assigned to the hydroxyl stretching [52]. Also, a strong peak at 1724 cm−1 (CO) which belongs to carboxylate groups, confirms the presence of GQDs in the membrane matrix [53].

Fig. 7b presents the results of XRD analysis of the fabricated nanofibrous membranes. XRD crystallographic measurements for the neat PVDF nanofibrous membrane showed two peaks at 18.6° and 20.6° which related to α (2 0 2) and β (2 0 0/1 1 0) crystal phases, confirming the existence of both α and β phases [54]. However, GQD3P nanofibrous membrane showed a weak broad pick at 26.2° (0 0 2) indicating the amorphous stacking structure of graphene which corresponds to the existence of GQDs nanoparticles in the nanofibrous membrane matrix.

Usually, the MD processes are being done under atmospheric pressure. So, there is no need to have membrane with high tensile characteristics; but in order to scale up the MD process and make modules, it would be required to have membrane with acceptable mechanical properties. The stress-strain diagrams for GQD3P and neat PVDF membranes are shown in Fig. 8 . The GQD3P membrane depicted elongation of 35% and a tensile strength of 3.533 MPa, meaning that the GQD3P membrane had rigid structure. Furthermore, the tensile strength and elongation for neat PVDF were 2.734 MPa and 89.3%, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance evaluation of PVDF and GQDs modified PVDF nanofibrous membranes in AGMD process
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the potential of the GQDs/PVDF nanofibrous membranes to produce fresh water, AGMD tests were performed to figure out the desalination ability and the resistance of fabricated membranes upon wetting phenomenon. Initial AGMD experiments were done for 8 h. As shown in Fig. 9 a, partial wetting occurred for the neat PVDF and GQD4P membranes after only 4 h of filtration. From the results, it can be concluded that the lower hydrophobicity and lower LEPw caused to partial wetting. The neat PVDF and GQD3P showed better water flux in 8 h experiments but the neat PVDF did not presented acceptable NaCl rejection because of the partial wetting (Fig. 9b). According to the short-term test results, GQD2P and GQD3P were selected to be compared with the neat PVDF membrane under 60 h experiment. Feed flow rate, feed temperature and coolant temperature were kept constant during the experiments on 30 L/h, 60 °C and 20 °C, respectively. Based on Fig. 9c, GQD3P membrane showed better performance in terms of salt rejection. As shown in Fig. 9d, GQD3P showed an initial flux value of 18.43 kg/m2 h which decreased to 12.26 kg/m2 h after 30 h, and then declined to 10.8 kg/m2 h after 60 h [55]. On the other hand, GQD2P showed an initial flux value of 12.98 kg/m2 h and decreased to 7.1 kg/m2 h after 30 h, then declined to 6.42 kg/m2 h after 60 h. This trend for water vapor flux can be attributed to higher LEPw of GQD3P membrane. As can be seen in Fig. 9c, the performance of GQD2P and GQD3P membranes was almost similar in case of NaCl rejection. However, GQD3P better performance could be due to pore blockage caused by higher concentration of nanoparticles, more compactness of fibers which lessen the distances among layers, and higher LEPw.

The flux and salt rejection of GQD3P nanofibrous membrane (GQD modified PVDF membrane with the best performance in this study) were compared with AGMD performance results of different membranes reported in the literatures (Table 3 ). The results shown in Table 3 prove that the GQD modified PVDF nanofibrous membrane has a good potential to apply in membrane distillation process due to its stability over long-term experiments, low nanoparticles content and high permeability and separation factor.

In summary, novel nanofibrous PVDF membranes with interesting properties for water desalination have been developed. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs)/polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) electrospun membranes were fabricated by electrospinning method and were tested in air gap membrane distillation (AGMD) process. It was found that the membranes possessed acceptable water contact angle, liquid entry pressure of water (LEPw) and porosity. The presence of GQDs was confirmed by FTIR while SEM and AFM analysis results exhibited the membrane structure became more compact and rougher after adding GQDs. Finally, a membrane sample with 0.25 wt.% GQDs represented a flux of about 10.8 kg/m2 h and salt rejection of about 99.7% over 60 h filtration of 3.5 wt.% NaCl solution as feed in AGMD process.
